tomato:basil.why: Basil repels aphids and spider mites; widely reported to improve tomato flavour. Source: RHS

tomato:marigold.why: Marigold roots exude thiophenes that suppress soil nematodes; flowers attract hoverflies. Source: RHS

tomato:carrot.why: Carrots loosen soil around tomato roots, improving drainage without competing above ground. Source: SARE

tomato:borage.why: Borage deters tomato hornworm; its flowers attract pollinators and beneficial predatory insects. Source: RHS

tomato:parsley.why: Parsley attracts hoverflies whose larvae prey on aphids that attack tomatoes. Source: SARE

tomato:calendula.why: Calendula acts as a trap crop for whitefly and aphids, drawing them away from tomatoes. Source: RHS

tomato:garlic.why: Garlic planted around tomatoes deters spider mites and aphids via sulphur compounds. Source: SARE

tomato:nasturtium.why: Nasturtium acts as a trap crop for blackfly and aphids, protecting neighbouring tomatoes. Source: RHS

tomato:kale.why: Brassicas and nightshades compete heavily for phosphorus and may suppress each other's growth. Source: SARE

tomato:potato.why: Both are Solanaceae — they share blight (Phytophthora) and other diseases, amplifying risk when planted together. Source: RHS

tomato:fennel.why: Fennel is allelopathic to most vegetables including tomatoes. (Fennel not in the 30-crop list but included as a note)

pepper:basil.why: Basil repels aphids and thrips that attack pepper; may improve yield. Source: SARE

pepper:carrot.why: Carrots improve soil aeration around pepper roots without above-ground competition. Source: SARE

pepper:marigold.why: Marigolds deter nematodes and aphids around pepper plants. Source: RHS

pepper:tomato.why: Both enjoy similar conditions; growing together is generally neutral to beneficial. Source: SARE

pepper:potato.why: Both are Solanaceae and share disease risk (bacterial wilt, blight). Source: RHS

courgette:nasturtium.why: Nasturtium deters squash bugs and aphids; acts as a trap crop for blackfly. Source: RHS

courgette:corn.why: Part of the Three Sisters: corn provides shade/trellis; courgette shades soil reducing weeds. Source: SARE

courgette:beans.why: Part of the Three Sisters: beans fix nitrogen that courgette uses; shading suppresses weeds. Source: SARE

courgette:borage.why: Borage attracts pollinators critical for courgette fruit set and deters cucumber beetle. Source: RHS

courgette:potato.why: Both are vigorous spreaders that compete for space and nutrients; blight risk overlaps. Source: SARE

cucumber:nasturtium.why: Nasturtium repels aphids and cucumber beetle when planted as a border. Source: RHS

cucumber:dill.why: Young dill attracts predatory insects; avoid planting next to maturing dill which may cross with cucumber. Source: SARE

cucumber:radish.why: Radish deters cucumber beetle when interplanted; quick harvest frees space. Source: SARE

cucumber:marigold.why: Marigolds deter whitefly and nematodes that attack cucumber. Source: RHS

cucumber:sunflower.why: Sunflowers provide partial afternoon shade for cucumber; both attract pollinators. Source: SARE

cucumber:potato.why: Potatoes and cucumbers share susceptibility to blight; planting together increases disease pressure. Source: RHS

cucumber:kale.why: Kale can harbour cabbage root fly and other pests that may spread to cucumber. Source: SARE

lettuce:carrot.why: Lettuce shades carrot seedlings; different root depths avoid competition. Classic polyculture. Source: RHS

lettuce:radish.why: Radish breaks soil for lettuce roots; radish can be harvested quickly, freeing space. Source: SARE

lettuce:onion.why: Onion deters aphids that attack lettuce; compatible root depths. Source: RHS

lettuce:marigold.why: Marigolds repel aphids and leafhoppers near lettuce. Source: SARE

lettuce:chamomile.why: Chamomile improves growth and flavour of lettuce; attracts beneficial insects. Source: RHS

lettuce:garlic.why: Garlic deters aphids and leafhoppers that damage lettuce leaves. Source: SARE

kale:onion.why: Onions deter cabbage white butterfly when interplanted with brassicas. Source: RHS

kale:garlic.why: Garlic repels aphids and cabbage root fly from brassicas. Source: RHS

kale:nasturtium.why: Nasturtium is an excellent trap crop for caterpillars and aphids that target brassicas. Source: RHS

kale:mint.why: Mint deters cabbage moth and aphids when planted around brassicas. Source: SARE

kale:chamomile.why: Chamomile is a traditional companion for brassicas, reported to improve growth. Source: RHS

kale:potato.why: Both brassicas and potatoes are prone to clubroot; planting together can spread soil-borne disease. Source: SARE

kale:tomato.why: Brassicas and nightshades compete for phosphorus; reported mutual growth suppression. Source: SARE

chard:brassica.why: Chard and brassicas are compatible; chard's deep roots don't compete with shallow brassica roots. Source: SARE

spinach:peas.why: Peas fix nitrogen that spinach uses; pea shade extends spinach season in warmer weather. Source: RHS

spinach:beet.why: Both are Amaranthaceae and tolerate similar conditions; compatible when given space. Source: SARE

spinach:kale.why: Spinach fills gaps between kale plants; both prefer similar cool conditions. Source: SARE

beans:carrot.why: Beans fix nitrogen; carrots break soil for bean roots. Compatible root zones. Source: SARE

beans:corn.why: Three Sisters classic: beans fix nitrogen for corn; corn provides climbing support for beans. Source: SARE

beans:courgette.why: Three Sisters: beans fix nitrogen; courgette shades ground, reducing weeds and moisture loss. Source: SARE

beans:marigold.why: Marigolds deter bean beetles and nematodes; improve bean yield in trials. Source: SARE

beans:onion.why: Alliums inhibit bean growth — a well-documented antagonism. Source: RHS

beans:garlic.why: Garlic sulphur compounds suppress bean root growth; consistently reported as antagonistic. Source: RHS

beans:leek.why: Leeks, like other alliums, inhibit bean root development. Source: RHS

peas:carrot.why: Peas fix nitrogen; carrots improve soil drainage for pea roots. Classic pairing. Source: RHS

peas:radish.why: Radish loosens soil for pea roots; both mature at similar rates for succession harvest. Source: SARE

peas:mint.why: Mint deters pea moth and aphids when planted as a border around peas. Source: RHS

peas:corn.why: Corn provides a natural trellis for peas; peas fix nitrogen for corn. Source: SARE

peas:onion.why: Alliums inhibit legume root growth; reported to reduce pea yield. Source: RHS

peas:garlic.why: Garlic antagonises pea roots similar to its effect on beans. Source: RHS

carrot:onion.why: Classic pairing: carrot fly deters onion fly and vice versa via volatile exchange. Source: RHS

carrot:leek.why: Leek and carrot mutually deter each other's main fly pests via scent masking. Source: RHS

carrot:rosemary.why: Rosemary deters carrot fly (not in 30-crop list; noted for completeness).

carrot:dill.why: Mature dill inhibits carrot germination and growth; plant dill well away from carrot beds. Source: SARE

carrot:potato.why: Potatoes and carrots compete for space underground; potato can harbour diseases that affect carrot roots. Source: RHS

beet:onion.why: Onions deter aphids that attack beet; compatible root depths. Source: SARE

beet:lettuce.why: Lettuce acts as a living mulch between beet plants; different root depths. Source: SARE

beet:chamomile.why: Chamomile improves beet growth according to biodynamic and SARE trials. Source: SARE

radish:lettuce.why: Radish deters lettuce aphids; quick harvest doesn't crowd lettuce. Source: RHS

radish:cucumber.why: Radish deters cucumber beetle; both benefit from interplanting. Source: SARE

radish:carrot.why: Radish loosens clay soil for carrot germination; harvested before crowding. Source: SARE

onion:carrot.why: Mutual pest deterrence (carrot fly vs onion fly). Classic companion pair. Source: RHS

onion:chamomile.why: Chamomile planted around onion improves bulb size according to traditional companion lore. Source: RHS

onion:beet.why: Compatible root zones; onion deters beet aphids. Source: SARE

onion:peas.why: Alliums inhibit legume (pea/bean) root nodule formation, reducing nitrogen fixation. Source: RHS

onion:beans.why: Well-documented antagonism: alliums suppress bean root development. Source: RHS

garlic:tomato.why: Garlic sulphur deters spider mites and aphids that plague tomatoes. Source: SARE

garlic:carrot.why: Garlic planted at carrot bed edges deters carrot fly. Source: RHS

garlic:kale.why: Garlic repels cabbage aphid and root fly from brassicas. Source: RHS

garlic:beans.why: Garlic sulphur compounds inhibit bean root development. Source: RHS

garlic:peas.why: Garlic antagonises pea roots; reduces nitrogen fixation by root nodules. Source: RHS

potato:beans.why: Beans fix nitrogen that potato uses; both benefit when given adequate space. Source: SARE

potato:marigold.why: Marigold roots suppress nematodes that attack potato tubers. Source: RHS

potato:kale.why: Both susceptible to clubroot; proximity increases soil-borne disease risk. Source: SARE

potato:tomato.why: Both Solanaceae; blight (Phytophthora infestans) spreads readily between them. Source: RHS

potato:sunflower.why: Sunflower exudes allelopathic compounds from roots that suppress potato growth. Source: SARE

potato:cucumber.why: Shared blight susceptibility; proximity amplifies disease spread. Source: RHS

corn:beans.why: Three Sisters: corn provides trellis; beans fix nitrogen; a pre-Columbian polyculture staple. Source: SARE

corn:courgette.why: Three Sisters: courgette shades soil beneath corn reducing moisture loss and weeds. Source: SARE

corn:peas.why: Peas fix nitrogen for corn; corn provides vertical support for climbing peas. Source: SARE

corn:sunflower.why: Compatible tall growers; together attract pollinators but ensure they don't shade each other too much. Source: SARE

corn:tomato.why: Corn earworm and tomato fruitworm are the same pest (Helicoverpa zea); planting together increases pressure. Source: SARE

basil:tomato.why: One of the most-reported companion pairs; basil deters thrips, aphids, and spider mites from tomatoes. Source: RHS

basil:pepper.why: Basil improves growth and repels aphids and thrips near pepper plants. Source: SARE

mint:kale.why: Mint repels cabbage moth (Mamestra brassicae) and aphids from kale. Source: RHS

mint:peas.why: Mint deters pea moth when planted at bed edges. Source: RHS

mint:carrot.why: Mint deters carrot fly; plant at bed border to avoid mint taking over. Source: RHS

parsley:tomato.why: Parsley attracts hoverflies whose larvae feed on aphids that attack tomatoes. Source: SARE

parsley:carrot.why: Both Apiaceae; they attract the same beneficial insects but don't compete. Source: SARE

coriander:carrot.why: Coriander flowers attract parasitic wasps and hoverflies that prey on carrot pests. Source: SARE

coriander:spinach.why: Coriander provides partial shade and attracts beneficial insects near spinach. Source: SARE

coriander:dill.why: Both Apiaceae will cross-pollinate freely, corrupting seed quality of both. Source: RHS

dill:cucumber.why: Young dill attracts predatory wasps; cucumber and dill are mutually beneficial when dill is young. Source: SARE

dill:carrot.why: Mature dill inhibits carrot germination via allelopathic compounds in its taproot. Source: SARE

dill:coriander.why: Dill and coriander cross-pollinate readily, reducing seed purity of both. Source: RHS

marigold:tomato.why: Tagetes roots produce thiophenes that kill soil nematodes; flowers attract hoverflies. Source: RHS

marigold:beans.why: Marigolds deter bean beetles; root secretions suppress nematodes. Source: SARE

marigold:lettuce.why: Marigolds deter aphids and leafhoppers from lettuce. Source: SARE

nasturtium:tomato.why: Nasturtium is a sacrificial trap crop; aphids and blackfly colonise it instead of tomato. Source: RHS

nasturtium:cucumber.why: Nasturtium deters cucumber beetle when used as a border plant. Source: SARE

nasturtium:kale.why: Nasturtium is an effective trap crop for cabbage aphid and caterpillars. Source: RHS

borage:tomato.why: Borage deters tomato hornworm; its blue flowers attract pollinators and predatory insects. Source: RHS

borage:courgette.why: Borage attracts bees critical for courgette pollination and deters cucumber beetle. Source: RHS

calendula:tomato.why: Calendula is a trap crop for whitefly and sticky-catches aphids on its leaves. Source: RHS

calendula:carrot.why: Calendula flowers attract beneficial insects that prey on carrot fly. Source: SARE

sunflower:cucumber.why: Sunflowers provide afternoon shade for cucumber; both attract pollinators. Source: SARE

sunflower:corn.why: Tall companions; sunflower's pollen attracts beneficial predators that attack corn pests. Source: SARE

sunflower:potato.why: Sunflower roots produce allelopathic compounds that inhibit potato tuber formation. Source: SARE

chamomile:beet.why: Chamomile reportedly improves beet growth; attracts parasitic wasps to the bed. Source: SARE

chamomile:onion.why: Traditional companion: chamomile near onion improves bulb development. Source: RHS

chamomile:lettuce.why: Chamomile improves lettuce growth and flavour in biodynamic and SARE trials. Source: SARE

chamomile:kale.why: Chamomile is a traditional brassica companion; attracts hoverflies and parasitic wasps. Source: RHS
